summary:
The city of Uruk, located on the ancient course of the Euphrates River in what is now southern Iraq, stands as the world's first true city — the largest human settlement on Earth during the fourth millennium BCE and the origin point of writing, monumental urban architecture, and complex administrative institutions. At its peak around 3200-3000 BCE, Uruk may have housed between 40,000 and 80,000 people within a settled urban core, surrounded by an outer ring of gardens, fields, and satellite villages, making it an unprecedented concentration of humanity with no parallel anywhere in the world at the time.

Uruk dominated the landscape of the [[event:1617|Uruk Period]] both physically and institutionally. The city was organized around two major sacred precincts. The Eanna precinct, dedicated to the goddess Inanna, housed the administrative heart of the city and produced most of the earliest written tablets. The [[event:1623|Eanna Temple Complex]] included a series of large halls, courtyards, and eventually the great ziggurat platform that dominated the city's skyline. A second precinct, the Anu Ziggurat, was crowned by the [[event:1624|White Temple]], an elaborate mudbrick sanctuary raised on a high terrace above the city.

Uruk's urban economy was managed through the temple institutions, which functioned as redistributive centers collecting agricultural products from surrounding villages and allocating rations to the craftspeople, laborers, and specialists who worked for the temple. This system required the invention of record-keeping, leading directly to the development of proto-cuneiform tablets. Thousands of tablets recording rations of beer and bread for workers, quantities of livestock, and deliveries of raw materials have been excavated from Uruk's administrative precincts.

The city's influence radiated far beyond its walls through the [[event:1622|Uruk Expansion]], a network of colonies, trading posts, and culturally influenced settlements that spread Uruk material culture across much of the ancient Near East. Long-distance trade brought cedar timber from the Levant, copper from Anatolia and Iran, lapis lazuli from Afghanistan, and gold from Egypt, all flowing through Uruk's distributive system. This trade network made Uruk not merely a city but the hub of the ancient world's first international exchange system.

The city's enormous scale and complexity effectively meant that urban life — with all its attendant specialization, anonymity, and institutional organization — was invented at Uruk. The traditions that Uruk pioneered: monumental temple architecture, bureaucratic record-keeping, standardized craft production, and long-distance trade, became the template for later Mesopotamian civilization and, through cultural diffusion, for complex societies far beyond.
